method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The island of Brecqhou, sometimes spelled Brechou, lies in the Channel Islands and is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[5]

Historically, the island bore a different name reflecting its ownership. When the Channel Islands formed a single Bailiwick, Brecqhou was part of the Fief of Vinchelez in Jersey, which was sold to Nicholas de Chesney and inherited by his son in 1326.[2] When Jeanne de Chesney married Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey, ownership of the island passed through a line of the Le Marchant family, and the island became known as Isle des Marchants, thus linking it to this family.[2] Even though ownership of the island subsequently passed to others, the name Isle des Marchants continued to be used.[2]

The name "Brecqhou" refers specifically to the island itself, whereas the name "Sark" is used to refer to both the island of Sark and, more broadly, to the combined territory of Sark, Brecqhou, and the other islets and rocks within Sark's jurisdiction.[1] In this context, the island of Sark is sometimes referred to as the "mainland", with Brecqhou considered part of the island's broader territory.[2]

==Geography==
Brecqhou is one of the Channel Islands, a group of islands located in the English Channel off the French coast of Normandy.[2] It lies within the Bailiwick of Guernsey, which also includes Guernsey itself, Alderney, Sark, Herm, Jethou, Burhou, and Lihou.[2][3]

The island is situated just off the western coast of Great Sark, from which it is separated by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[4] Sark, which lies approximately 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France, has a total area, including Brecqhou, of 2.1 square miles (5.5 square km).[4]

Geologically, Brecqhou belongs to the oldest rock formations found in the Channel Islands, which are confined to the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[5] Most of Brecqhou, like much of Sark and the southern part of Guernsey, is composed of metamorphic rocks, principally schists and gneisses.[5] These rocks have undergone several phases of deformation and metamorphism during early Proterozoic time, approximately 2020 million years ago.[5]

Brecqhou is a privately owned island with only a tiny population in comparison with Sark.[1] The separation of Brecqhou from Sark by the waters of Le Gouliot Channel, together with the island's private status, has contributed to the complex and sometimes contested perceptions of jurisdiction between the two islands.[1]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Following the purchase of the tenement of Brecqhou by Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay in 1993 for £2.3 million, the twins commissioned the construction of a large castellated residence on the island known as Fort Brecqhou.[2] Described as the largest house built in Britain for at least two centuries, the building was designed by the British classical architect John Quinlan Terry and constructed between 1994 and 1996.[2][1]

The house is a granite-clad castle built on a quadrangular plan in the castellated Gothic style, featuring circular turrets at each of its four corners.[2] It also incorporates giant twisted chimneys in the style developed during the reign of Henry VIII, a chimney form considered unique to English architecture.[2] The estimated cost of building the house, gardens, and outbuildings was around £90 million.[2]

Fort Brecqhou was constructed from two types of Spanish granite, giving the structure a near-indestructible quality.[1] The architectural historian David Watkin praised the castle's dramatic roofscape, with its perilous platforms behind crenelated parapets offering expansive views across sky and sea.[1] Sitting atop a crown of rock on the island adjacent to Sark, the fortress-like residence has been seen as symbolising both the Barclays' considerable wealth and their well-known desire for privacy.[1] Following the death of Sir David Barclay in January 2021, the house was reported to have "fallen silent."[1]

==Flag and stamps==
The private ownership and contested jurisdictional status of Brecqhou in relation to Sark have been reflected in symbolic gestures such as the adoption of a distinctive flag and the issuing of stamps associated with the island.[2]

As part of the wider Bailiwick of Guernsey, Brecqhou falls within the postal jurisdiction that gained independence from the British postal system in 1969, when Guernsey began issuing its own distinctive stamps.[5] The Bailiwick's postal history, which extends back to informal 15th-century letter exchanges and the formal establishment of a post office in St Peter Port in 1794, provides the broader framework within which postal matters concerning Brecqhou are situated.[5]

The island's separate identity within the Sark/Brecqhou dyad, and the tensions that have arisen between the owners of Brecqhou and the jurisdiction of Sark, have contributed to the symbolic assertion of Brecqhou's distinctiveness through such emblems.[2][4]

==Public visits==
Since its purchase by the Barclay brothers in 1993, access to Brecqhou has been restricted, with visitation to the island permitted by invitation only.[4] This private status stands in contrast to other islands in the Bailiwick of Guernsey, such as Herm, which is the smallest of the Channel Islands open to the public.[1]

The island's north coast features infrastructure associated with this restricted access, including a slipway with high-pressure sodium security lights and lighting for a helicopter landing area.[4] At night, at least five light sources from Brecqhou can be seen from the north end of Sark, although a 10:30 pm light curfew is in place on the island.[4] The population of Brecqhou is unknown and consists of the Barclay family's residence along with a small number of island employees.[2]

==Notes and references==
Brecqhou is one of the eight Channel Islands, forming part of a small archipelago at the entrance to the gulf of St Malo, some 80 miles off the southern coast of England.[1] It is the smallest of the eight islands, which in descending order of size are Jersey, Guernsey, Alderney, Sark, Herm, Jethou, Lihou and Brecqhou.[1]

The island covers approximately 160 acres and lies just 85 metres from Sark, separated by a narrow channel.[2] Like Sark, Brecqhou is surrounded by steep cliffs, making landing difficult except at its harbour.[2] In legal and political terms, the States of Guernsey has noted that "the islands of Herm, Jethou and Lihou are part of Guernsey and the island of Brecqhou is part of Sark".[2]

A sense of Brecqhou's physical, social and cultural dislocation from Sark became particularly evident in the 1960s, when Leonard Matchan, then owner of the Brecqhou tenement, created a Brecqhou flag based on the Sark flag of 1938, with the addition of his own coat of arms.[2] Matchan also issued stamps (more precisely, carriage labels) in 1969, and further issues have been produced by the island's subsequent owners.[2]

The current owners of Brecqhou, Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay, have been described as exerting considerable financial influence in the region, and are regarded by some Sark residents as posing a threat to Sark's environment, institutions and way of life.[3] After an unsuccessful legal challenge in 1996, ongoing disputes between the Barclay brothers and the Sark authorities have shaped debate over the island's governance.[3] A large mansion built by the Barclay brothers is a prominent feature of Brecqhou's coastline.[5]

Cartographically, the Ordnance Survey of Britain does not cover the Channel Islands, but Brecqhou is included on the 2011 1:15000/1:10000 map of the Bailiwick of Guernsey produced by the States of Guernsey, which also encompasses Guernsey, Sark, Herm, Alderney and Jethou.[4] The island is visible from the Gouliot headland on Sark, which offers splendid views across the narrow channel towards Brecqhou.[5]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
In 1565, Queen Elizabeth I issued Letters Patent to Helier de Carteret of Jersey, granting him the Seignory of Sark as a "fief haubert", the highest form of fief under the feudal property system of the time.[2] Under this arrangement, the Seigneur owed homage to the Monarch in person and was required to supply a "knight's service" when called upon, translating to a fully equipped knight with hauberk, helmet, shield and full armour.[2] In return for paying the Queen "one twentieth of a knight's fee", de Carteret was granted extensive rights to the land and surrounding sea, along with near-complete legal and political power, and was charged with keeping the island "free of the Queen's enemies".[2]

One of the conditions of the grant was that de Carteret should protect Sark with 40 armed men, and given the close proximity of Brecqhou, it has been argued that this defensive obligation extended to Brecqhou as well, so that just as Sark has jurisdiction over its surrounding islets, reefs and rocks, Brecqhou likewise forms part of Sark's fief.[1] However, Rivett (1999) has argued that Brecqhou was not originally part of the fief, particularly because it was not a tenement, and was only later claimed as such.[1] Dawes has similarly suggested that Brecqhou is not a "tenement of Sark", a comment implying an historical positioning of the jurisdictional status of the island when its governance was earlier contested.[4] Rivett further contends that Dame Sibyl Hathaway illegally bestowed upon Brecqhou the status of a Sark tenement together with a seat in Chief Pleas.[4]

Despite these disputes, the position that Brecqhou falls within Sark's jurisdiction has been reinforced at senior levels of political authority, with the UK Ministry of Justice stating that "Within the Bailiwick of Guernsey there are three separate jurisdictions: Guernsey (which includes the islands of Herm and Jethou); Alderney; and Sark (which includes the island of Brecqhou)", a view echoed by the British-Irish Council.[1] Legal advice provided in connection with the 1949 sale of Brecqhou described the tenure of Brecqhou as similar to that of other tenements in Sark, with the owner holding as tenant in perpetuity, although an advocate overseeing the sale noted that "strictly speaking, the owner of the above Island is called tenant, but in effect, he enjoys a freehold holding, although legally speaking, he has but a manorial freehold".[4]

As a fief, Sark's legislative body is Chief Pleas, and because Brecqhou is treated as a tenement of the fief, the laws of Sark generally apply to Brecqhou; however, certain laws have been disapplied to Brecqhou, giving the island a special status and a degree of autonomy that in some respects privileges it over other tenements.[1] Helier de Carteret originally divided the land of Sark into forty tenements, each held in perpetuity with reciprocal duties of defence and tithe.[3]

Tensions over this feudal relationship intensified after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay purchased Brecqhou in 1993 and invested heavily in constructing a castle and gardens there.[3] Under Sark's tax laws, the Barclays were liable to pay the *treizième*, one-thirteenth of the purchase price, directly to the Seigneur, a sum estimated at £179,000, and invoking human rights principles they began a sustained campaign of legal action against seigneurial feudalism.[3] They succeeded first in overturning Sark's male-dominated inheritance laws, and in 2007 their pressure forced the Seigneur and Chief Pleas to abolish the *treizième*, replacing it with a property transfer tax payable to the island.[3] In 2008, Sark's feudal government was replaced with an elected assembly, ending the hereditary rule of the Seigneur that had existed since the 16th century.[1] The Barclays' broader campaign culminated in the Privy Council's decision to call time on Sark's feudal regime and introduce universal voting rights and a 28-member assembly, a reform widely portrayed as a democratic victory.[5]
